questions.text: I rarely pick up on the implicit rules of a new social group without being told.
I tend to think of my career as the central project of my life.
I believe my emotional steadiness allows me to remain functional in situations that would overwhelm a more reactive person.
I notice when someone adopts a casual posture to deliberately lower the perceived stakes of a conversation.
I believe that every interaction contains far more information than the words exchanged.
I am comfortable making decisions that cause short-term distress to others when I believe the outcome is better overall.
I experience fear as a measured alertness rather than a full-body panic.
I am inclined to share my reasoning first and let the group reach the decision.
My body keeps score of every emotional event — I feel the accumulated weight.
I think about the second- and third-order consequences of my decisions.
I deal with problems as they come up rather than trying to anticipate every possible issue.
I hold the view that every skill I develop should eventually be taught to someone else.
I tend to spend my weekends doing things for other people rather than pursuing my own hobbies.
I am inclined to avoid exercising authority if it would create tension.
I let habits form and dissolve naturally without deliberate auditing.
I do not pick up on when someone is testing my reaction before committing to a stance.
I feel that the pull toward responding to suffering is a moral instinct that I trust more than almost any other guide.
Loud noises, bright lights, or sudden changes can trigger an outsized emotional response in me.
I find that cooking for others, even when I am tired, fills me with purpose.
I hold the view that moral engagement with suffering requires me to remain attentive to it long after the initial awareness has passed.
I feel that the recognition of suffering as important is the essential first step without which no meaningful response can follow.
I complete the hardest tasks first when my energy is highest.
I find that I pay more attention to what is said than to the unspoken dynamics in a conversation.
I hold the view that the most important relationship I have is the one with myself.
I notice when a person arranges their belongings on a table to claim space or establish boundaries.
I gravitate toward being the one who decides when a discussion has gone on long enough.
My emotions don't trickle in — they arrive like a sudden downpour.
I instinctively place myself in the center of the information flow.
I act on my initial understanding and ask for clarification only if I get stuck.
I hold the view that a rational, evidence-based approach to human distress is the method that minimizes unintended consequences and maximizes positive impact.
I notice that my emotional reactions rarely interfere with my ability to function.
I find that my natural inclination toward analytical thinking during emotional situations is an asset I have learned to rely on.
I tend to set the criteria by which the group evaluates options.
I feel a genuine sense of accomplishment when I execute a plan exactly as designed.
I tend to choose the restaurant, the movie, or the activity that I most want.
I bring concerns about a plan to the group rather than canceling it myself.
